Overall, the recurring theme is a possible reduction in later residential care placement, but with limited certainty.
contradict_summary: No direct contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because the available reviews consistently report fewer patients in residential/institutional care after hospital-at-home compared with inpatient care. However, confidence is only moderate at best because the evidence is low-certainty and comes from early discharge hospital-at-home rather than direct admission-avoidance hospital-at-home.

claim: For older people referred for acute hospital admission, admission avoidance hospital at home may improve satisfaction with care and probably reduces health service costs compared with inpatient hospital care.
support_summary: Two Cochrane reviews specifically on admission-avoidance hospital at home report that patients had higher satisfaction with home-based care and that, in the limited economic evaluations available, costs were lower than inpatient care when informal care costs were excluded (PMID: 18843751). The newer update concludes that admission-avoidance hospital at home may be an effective alternative for a selected group of older patients, but the abstract provided does not clearly report satisfaction or cost results in detail, so its support for those specific outcomes is indirect from the abstract alone (PMID: 27583824). Overall, the supporting evidence is mainly driven by systematic review evidence in older/mixed acute medical populations, but it also notes limitations from small trials and imprecision.
contradict_summary: The contradicting items are all Cochrane reviews of hospital-at-home models, but they primarily evaluate early-discharge hospital at home rather than admission-avoidance, making them only partially applicable to the claim and a notable discrepancy (PMID: 28651296; PMID: 19160179; PMID: 16034853). Across these reviews, patient satisfaction is reported as somewhat better with hospital at home, but the evidence for cost reduction is described as uncertain, mixed, or lacking sufficient objective evidence of economic benefit (PMID: 28651296; PMID: 19160179; PMID: 16034853). Some reviews also note increased readmissions in older patients with mixed conditions, which may weaken claims of overall service advantage, though this is not the same as directly refuting satisfaction or cost reduction (PMID: 19160179; PMID: 16034853). Thus, the contradiction centers mostly on the cost component and on indirectness of population/intervention rather than on clear evidence that satisfaction is worse.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest and most directly relevant evidence comes from admission-avoidance systematic reviews showing improved patient satisfaction and some indication of lower health service costs, whereas the contradictory evidence is less directly applicable because it largely concerns early-discharge hospital at home and mainly challenges the certainty of cost savings rather than the satisfaction benefit.

claim: Among people with cancer, physical interventions for return to work likely increase return to work compared with usual care.
support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
contradict_summary: Two Cochrane reviews specifically addressing cancer and return-to-work report that physical training/physical interventions were not more effective than usual care for improving return to work, with imprecise effect estimates from a very small evidence base (OR/RR about 1.20 with wide confidence intervals) and low or very low certainty evidence (PMID: 21328297; PMID: 26405010). These same reviews found benefit for multidisciplinary interventions that included physical components, but that does not directly support physical-only interventions and creates an important distinction from the claim (PMID: 21328297; PMID: 26405010). A broader review of workplace interventions found that the single cancer study did not show a considerable effect on lasting return to work (HR 0.88, 95% CI 0.53 to 1.47), though this concerns workplace interventions rather than clearly defined physical interventions, so its relevance is somewhat indirect to the claim (PMID: 26436959). Overall, the recurring theme is absence of demonstrated benefit for physical-only or cancer-specific workplace interventions versus usual care, with low-certainty and limited data.
conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. There is no direct supporting evidence here, while two cancer-specific systematic reviews indicate that physical interventions alone have not shown better return-to-work outcomes than usual care, although the certainty is limited and multidisciplinary interventions may still help.

claim: Among people with cancer, multidisciplinary interventions for return to work likely increase return to work compared with usual care.
support_summary: Two Cochrane reviews support the claim that multidisciplinary return-to-work interventions improve work outcomes in people with cancer. The newer review found moderate-quality evidence from five RCTs that multidisciplinary programs combining vocational, psycho-educational, and physical components increased return-to-work versus usual care (RR 1.11, 95% CI 1.03 to 1.16; n=450) (PMID: 26405010). The earlier review likewise reported moderate-quality evidence that multidisciplinary interventions with physical, psychological, and vocational components improved return-to-work compared with usual care (OR 1.87, 95% CI 1.07 to 3.27) (PMID: 21328297). Both reviews also noted that other intervention types, such as psycho-educational/psychological or physical interventions alone, did not clearly improve return-to-work, reinforcing that the positive signal is specific to multidisciplinary approaches (PMID: 26405010, PMID: 21328297).
contradict_summary: One Cochrane review is listed as contradicting, but its abstract addresses workplace interventions across mixed conditions rather than multidisciplinary cancer-specific return-to-work programs, so it is not a clean test of the claim (PMID: 26436959). In the cancer subgroup, based on only one study, workplace intervention did not considerably reduce time to lasting return-to-work (HR 0.88, 95% CI 0.53 to 1.47), and the authors judged the cancer evidence low quality and not showing a clear effect (PMID: 26436959). This creates some uncertainty, but the apparent contradiction is partly a scope mismatch because 'workplace interventions' are not necessarily the same as the multidisciplinary interventions described in the supporting cancer-specific reviews (PMID: 26436959).
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The strongest and most directly relevant evidence comes from two cancer-specific Cochrane reviews showing moderate-quality benefit of multidisciplinary interventions on return-to-work, whereas the contradictory item is lower-directness evidence based on a single cancer study of workplace interventions with a somewhat different intervention scope.

claim: Among people with cancer, psycho-educational interventions for return to work probably make little or no difference to return to work compared with usual care.
support_summary: A Cochrane review update found two studies of psycho-educational interventions in cancer patients and reported low-quality evidence of similar return-to-work rates versus usual care (RR 1.09, 95% CI 0.88 to 1.35; n=260), with no quality-of-life difference, directly supporting the claim that these interventions make little or no difference (PMID: 26405010). The same review distinguished this from multidisciplinary interventions, which did show improved return-to-work, underscoring that the null finding was specific to psycho-educational interventions rather than all intervention types (PMID: 26405010).
contradict_summary: The only cited contradicting item appears miscategorized based on its abstract. An earlier Cochrane review reported low-quality evidence of similar return-to-work rates for psychological interventions compared with usual care (OR 2.32, 95% CI 0.94 to 5.71), which does not demonstrate a clear benefit because the confidence interval includes no effect (PMID: 21328297). That review also found benefit only for multidisciplinary interventions, not for psychological interventions alone, so it does not meaningfully contradict the claim and instead is broadly consistent with little or no difference for psycho-educational/psychological approaches (PMID: 21328297).
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The best direct evidence is a systematic review showing low-quality evidence of no important difference in return-to-work for psycho-educational interventions versus usual care, while the supposed contradicting study appears to be misclassified and is not clearly inconsistent with the claim.

claim: In adults with heart failure, exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation compared with no exercise control does not reduce all-cause mortality in the short term.
support_summary: Multiple systematic reviews and meta-analyses in adults with heart failure consistently report no significant short-term reduction in all-cause mortality with exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation versus no-exercise control. The strongest direct evidence comes from successive Cochrane reviews showing little or no short-term mortality effect: RR 0.89 (95% CI 0.66 to 1.21) in 44 trials (PMID: 30695817), RR 0.93 (95% CI 0.69 to 1.27) in 33 trials (PMID: 24771460), and no significant difference in trials with <1 year follow-up in an earlier review (PMID: 20393935). A 2023 Cochrane update likewise found no short-term mortality impact (RR 0.93, 95% CI 0.71 to 1.21) across 60 trials, while showing benefits for hospitalization and quality of life (PMID: 37850321). Additional meta-analytic evidence is concordant, including a review/meta-analysis reporting no reduction in all-cause mortality (RR 0.89, 95% CI 0.66 to 1.21) (PMID: 31302050) and an IPD meta-analysis finding no statistically significant mortality benefit (HR 0.83, 95% CI 0.67 to 1.04) (PMID: 31140973). One broader vascular-disease review also found little or no mortality effect with exercise, but it is only indirectly relevant because it was not heart-failure-specific (PMID: 40630612).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided. The supplied evidence set contains no abstract-level study directly showing that exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation reduces short-term all-cause mortality in adults with heart failure compared with no exercise control.
conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. This judgement is based on multiple large, consistent systematic reviews/meta-analyses—especially repeated Cochrane reviews—showing no statistically significant reduction in short-term all-cause mortality, with no direct contradictory evidence provided.

claim: In adults with heart failure, exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation compared with no exercise control likely reduces all-cause hospital admissions and heart failure-related hospital admissions in the short term.
support_summary: Multiple systematic reviews/meta-analyses of randomized trials in adults with heart failure consistently report short-term reductions in hospital admissions with exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation versus no exercise control. The strongest and most directly relevant evidence comes from Cochrane and related meta-analyses: all-cause hospitalization was reduced in the short term with RR about 0.69-0.75, and HF-specific hospitalization was also reduced in earlier reviews with RR about 0.59-0.61 (PMID: 30695817, PMID: 24771460, PMID: 31302050). An older Cochrane review also reported reduced hospitalization, including systolic HF-related admissions, though the abstract is less quantitative and more cautious (PMID: 20393935). The 2023 Cochrane update confirms reduced short-term all-cause hospitalization across center-, home-, and hybrid delivery models, but its abstract does not explicitly report HF-specific hospitalization, so it only partially supports the full claim (PMID: 37850321). Two listed support items are only indirectly relevant because they concern coronary heart disease or mixed vascular disease rather than heart failure, so they should be treated as weak/miscategorized support rather than direct evidence (PMID: 41077559, PMID: 40630612).
contradict_summary: One individual participant data meta-analysis of 19 RCTs found no statistically significant reduction with exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation in either all-cause hospitalization (HR 0.90, 95% CI 0.76-1.06) or HF-related hospitalization (HR 0.98, 95% CI 0.72-1.35) versus no-exercise control, despite improvements in exercise capacity and quality of life (PMID: 31140973). This is direct contradictory evidence because it evaluates the same population and outcomes as the claim. However, the abstract also notes limitations in outcome definition/collection and incomplete IPD availability, which may temper certainty.
conclusion: Overall, the claim is more supported than contradicted. Several large systematic reviews/meta-analyses of randomized trials in heart failure consistently report short-term reductions in all-cause hospitalization and usually HF-specific hospitalization, whereas the main contradictory evidence is a single IPD meta-analysis finding no significant hospitalization benefit.

claim: In adults with heart failure, exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation compared with no exercise control likely improves health-related quality of life in the short term.
support_summary: Multiple systematic reviews and meta-analyses of randomized trials in adults with heart failure consistently report better short-term health-related quality of life with exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation versus no-exercise control, typically using the Minnesota Living with Heart Failure Questionnaire, with pooled mean differences around 6-7 points favoring exercise (PMID: 24771460, PMID: 30695817, PMID: 31302050, PMID: 37850321). An individual participant data meta-analysis also found a statistically significant HRQoL benefit at 12 months with exercise-based rehabilitation versus control (PMID: 31140973). More recent HFpEF-focused evidence likewise found improved disease-specific and generic HRQoL over a median 16 weeks, though with substantial heterogeneity and caution about trial quality and size (PMID: 40900142). Reviews also suggest these HRQoL benefits are seen across different rehabilitation delivery modes, including home-, centre-, and hybrid-based programs (PMID: 35680170, PMID: 37850321). Two listed supportive studies are only indirectly relevant because they address broader vascular disease or coronary heart disease rather than heart failure specifically, so they should be interpreted as peripheral support rather than direct evidence (PMID: 40630612, PMID: 41077559).
contradict_summary: The only contradicting item appears misclassified for this claim: it studied heart transplant recipients rather than adults with heart failure and therefore does not directly test the target population (PMID: 28375548). In that separate population, the review found no short-term HRQoL benefit from exercise-based rehabilitation, but this does not meaningfully contradict the heart-failure-specific claim (PMID: 28375548). No direct heart-failure evidence in the contradiction set shows that exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation fails to improve short-term HRQoL.
conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. Direct heart-failure-specific randomized evidence, including several Cochrane/systematic reviews and an IPD meta-analysis, consistently shows short-term HRQoL improvement with exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation versus no-exercise control, whereas the lone contradicting study is in heart transplant recipients and is not directly applicable.

claim: In adults with advanced non-small cell lung cancer receiving first-line chemotherapy, adding the TG4010 vaccine may slightly improve progression-free survival but probably makes little or no difference in overall survival or serious treatment-related adverse events compared with chemotherapy alone.
support_summary: One randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase 2b trial found that adding TG4010 to first-line chemotherapy in stage IV NSCLC modestly prolonged median progression-free survival (5.9 vs 5.1 months; HR 0.74, 95% CI 0.55-0.98), which is consistent with a slight PFS benefit (PMID: 26727163). The same abstract reports no grade 3/4 or serious adverse events attributed to TG4010 alone, and no serious adverse event related to the combination with chemotherapy, while severe treatment-related events with combined study treatments were not increased in the TG4010 arm versus placebo. However, the abstract does not report overall survival results, so support for the claim's "probably little or no difference in overall survival" component is indirect rather than directly demonstrated.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The available randomized trial directly supports a small progression-free survival benefit and suggests no increase in serious treatment-related adverse events, but it does not provide abstract-level overall survival data, so support is only moderate rather than strong.

claim: In adults with advanced non-small cell lung cancer receiving maintenance treatment after chemotherapy, the racotumomab vaccine may improve overall survival compared with placebo, while making little or no difference in progression-free survival or serious treatment-related adverse events.
support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

claim: In adults with advanced non-small cell lung cancer receiving chemotherapy, adding the SRL172 vaccine may not improve overall survival and may increase serious treatment-related adverse events compared with chemotherapy alone.
support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

claim: In people undergoing elective stoma reversal, purse-string skin closure likely reduces surgical site infection compared with linear skin closure.
support_summary: The supporting literature is extensive and consistent, with multiple meta-analyses and systematic reviews reporting markedly lower SSI rates with purse-string closure versus linear/conventional closure after stoma reversal, including pooled estimates around OR/RR 0.08-0.16 across RCT-focused and mixed-study analyses (PMID: 23354904, PMID: 26163961, PMID: 29474885, PMID: 30074070, PMID: 30809775, PMID: 30547183, PMID: 39213201, PMID: 40188299, PMID: 41486907). Several randomized and prospective/retrospective comparative studies also found significantly fewer SSIs with purse-string closure, often with large absolute differences, in adult, mixed, pediatric, ileostomy, and colostomy populations (PMID: 25285695, PMID: 29149428, PMID: 29657907, PMID: 33819218, PMID: 34546402, PMID: 36405661, PMID: 37978002, PMID: 38676785, PMID: 24035923, PMID: 24407267, PMID: 20703459, PMID: 30588593, PMID: 32476717). A recurring theme is that purse-string closure improves cosmesis/patient satisfaction while generally not changing hospital stay or operative time, though some studies note longer wound-healing/epithelialization time (PMID: 23354904, PMID: 25285695, PMID: 30588593). Some support items are weaker or indirect because they are risk-factor/design papers rather than direct head-to-head efficacy studies (PMID: 36002762, PMID: 31907033), but they do not materially alter the overall pattern. Overall, the abstract-level evidence strongly supports that purse-string closure likely reduces SSI in elective stoma reversal.
contradict_summary: The contradicting evidence is limited and mixed. One randomized trial reported a numerically higher SSI rate with purse-string closure (30% vs 15%), but the difference was not statistically significant, so it suggests uncertainty rather than a definitive harmful effect (PMID: 38186536). Other studies in this group actually showed numerically lower SSI rates with purse-string closure but concluded there was no significant difference between techniques, including a small comparative study (5.6% vs 16.7%, P=0.26), a multi-institutional retrospective analysis (2.7% vs 6.9%, P=0.25), and an RCT comparing purse-string to primary closure with drain (5.0% vs 8.9%, P=0.35) that favored the comparator mainly on wound-healing outcomes rather than SSI (PMID: 21602963, PMID: 37567817, PMID: 30367238). This creates a discrepancy in the CONTRADICT set because several included abstracts do not truly contradict the claim on direction of effect; they mainly fail to confirm superiority statistically. The main theme on this side is underpowered or comparator-specific studies finding no significant SSI difference, plus one outlier trial with a nonsignificant trend against purse-string closure.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The evidence base includes numerous meta-analyses and many comparative studies, most showing substantially lower SSI with purse-string closure, whereas the opposing evidence is sparse and mostly reflects nonsignificant or comparator-specific findings rather than clear evidence that linear closure is better.

claim: In people undergoing elective stoma reversal, purse-string skin closure may increase patient satisfaction compared with linear skin closure.
support_summary: Multiple studies, including randomized trials and meta-analyses, report higher patient satisfaction or better cosmetic satisfaction with purse-string closure versus linear/conventional closure after elective stoma reversal. Meta-analyses found improved satisfaction/cosmetic outcomes with purse-string closure alongside lower SSI rates (PMID: 30074070, PMID: 23354904, PMID: 26163961). Several primary studies also directly reported significantly higher satisfaction, better scar/cosmesis scores, or better overall wound satisfaction with purse-string closure (PMID: 36405661, PMID: 33819218, PMID: 32476717, PMID: 29657907). Some supporting items are only indirect for this specific claim because they measured scar quality/cosmesis rather than satisfaction itself (PMID: 37978002, PMID: 38676785), and one study in the support set actually found numerically higher SSI with purse-string closure and did not directly show satisfaction benefit in its own results, so its categorization is discrepant (PMID: 38186536). Overall, the main recurring theme is better patient-reported scar/cosmetic outcomes and satisfaction with purse-string closure, often accompanying lower SSI rates.
contradict_summary: Three contradicting items suggest that purse-string closure does not clearly improve patient satisfaction despite reducing SSI. Two randomized trials found no significant difference in patient satisfaction between purse-string and linear/conventional closure after stoma reversal (PMID: 29149428, PMID: 25285695). A systematic review reported lower satisfaction with purse-string closure during the first postoperative week, but no difference at 1 and 6 months, indicating any disadvantage may be transient and longer-term satisfaction may be similar (PMID: 30547183). Thus, the main contradictory theme is not that purse-string worsens satisfaction overall, but that its satisfaction advantage is inconsistent or absent across some trials.
conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. The balance of evidence includes multiple randomized studies and several meta-analyses showing higher patient/cosmetic satisfaction with purse-string closure, although some trials and one review indicate no long-term satisfaction difference, so the benefit appears supported but not completely uniform across studies.

claim: In people undergoing elective stoma reversal, purse-string skin closure may have little or no effect on incisional hernia or operative time compared with linear skin closure.
support_summary: Multiple meta-analyses and systematic reviews consistently report no statistically significant difference between purse-string and linear/conventional skin closure for incisional hernia and operative time after stoma reversal, while showing lower surgical site infection with purse-string closure (PMID: 30547183, PMID: 30074070, PMID: 39213201, PMID: 29474885, PMID: 41486907). The most directly relevant reviews include randomized trials and larger pooled samples, with one review of 8 RCTs finding similar incisional hernia rates and operative time between groups (PMID: 30547183) and another meta-analysis with trial sequential analysis also finding no difference for these outcomes (PMID: 30074070). A recent meta-analysis likewise reported no significant differences in operative time or incisional hernia despite lower SSI with purse-string closure (PMID: 39213201). One small comparative study supports similar operative time but did not report incisional hernia, so it only partially supports the claim (PMID: 21602963).
contradict_summary: One retrospective single-center study found a significantly lower CT-detected incisional hernia rate after purse-string closure versus direct stitch/standard closure at 12-24 months, suggesting purse-string closure may reduce hernia risk rather than having little or no effect (PMID: 29523989). However, this study is observational, smaller than the pooled meta-analytic evidence, and its comparator is described as direct stitch or standard closure, which may not perfectly align with all definitions of linear closure used in the claim. This creates a discrepancy with the broader meta-analytic literature that generally found no significant hernia difference.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. Several meta-analyses, including reviews of randomized trials, consistently found little or no difference in incisional hernia or operative time between purse-string and linear/conventional closure, whereas the contradictory evidence is limited to a single retrospective study reporting fewer hernias with purse-string closure.

claim: In women undergoing surgical repair of vaginal prolapse, permanent transvaginal mesh, compared with native tissue repair, reduces awareness of prolapse and recurrent prolapse on examination and lowers repeat surgery for prolapse.
support_summary: A high-quality Cochrane review of 37 RCTs found that, versus native tissue repair, permanent transvaginal mesh was associated with less subjective awareness of prolapse (RR 0.66), less recurrent prolapse on examination (RR 0.40), and fewer repeat operations specifically for prolapse (RR 0.53), although it increased other adverse outcomes and composite reoperation including mesh exposure (PMID: 26858090). Earlier review evidence also reported lower anterior-compartment recurrence on examination with polypropylene mesh than native tissue repair, though subjective and reoperation outcomes were limited or not clearly improved in that summary (PMID: 21927941). A systematic review likewise concluded that synthetic nonabsorbable mesh improved anatomic and bulge-symptom outcomes, mainly in the anterior compartment, compared with native tissue repair (PMID: 27275813). In women undergoing surgery for recurrent prolapse, an observational study reported lower anatomic recurrence, fewer bulge symptoms, and fewer prolapse reoperations in the mesh group, but total reoperation was similar once mesh-complication surgeries were counted (PMID: 27324758).
contradict_summary: Several contradicting items are actually mixed rather than purely contradictory: some reviews still report that polypropylene mesh reduces awareness and/or anatomic recurrence versus native tissue, but emphasize higher morbidity or that the overall risk-benefit profile does not support routine mesh use, especially for anterior, posterior, or apical prolapse subtypes (PMID: 27901278; PMID: 23633316). The strongest direct contradiction comes from later randomized and pragmatic studies showing no meaningful improvement in prolapse symptoms or quality of life with mesh augmentation in primary or repeat surgery, and in some long-term follow-up studies no reduction in subjective recurrence or repeat prolapse surgery despite lower anatomic recurrence in some cases (PMID: 28010989; PMID: 28052810; PMID: 32141709; PMID: 29167974; PMID: 31506808). Long-term recurrent-prolapse trials found similar composite success or subjective outcomes between mesh and native tissue, with mesh groups often having more additional operations when mesh exposures/complications were included (PMID: 29167974; PMID: 36662272). Large observational cohorts also associated transvaginal mesh for prolapse with increased repeat prolapse surgery and later complications compared with non-mesh repair in some compartments, especially anterior/posterior or apical repairs (PMID: 28010993; PMID: 30327850; PMID: 27902825). For apical and posterior prolapse specifically, Cochrane reviews concluded that limited evidence does not support transvaginal mesh over native tissue repair (PMID: 37493538; PMID: 27696355; PMID: 29502352).
conclusion: The statement is only partially supported overall and is substantially challenged by later and compartment-specific evidence. While earlier RCT-based syntheses support reduced awareness, objective recurrence, and prolapse-specific reoperation with permanent transvaginal mesh, more recent long-term trials and large observational studies frequently show no subjective benefit and no reduction—or even increases—in repeat surgery once mesh-related reinterventions and broader real-world outcomes are considered.

claim: In women undergoing surgical repair of vaginal prolapse, permanent transvaginal mesh, compared with native tissue repair, increases total repeat surgery, bladder injury, and de novo stress urinary incontinence.
support_summary: The strongest supporting evidence is a large Cochrane review of 37 RCTs showing that permanent transvaginal mesh versus native tissue repair increased the combined repeat-surgery outcome for prolapse/stress incontinence/mesh exposure, as well as bladder injury and de novo stress urinary incontinence (PMID: 26858090). Earlier Cochrane synthesis and RCT data also reported higher reoperation rates, de novo stress urinary incontinence, and/or bladder perforation/bladder injury with polypropylene transvaginal mesh compared with native tissue repair, especially for anterior or multicompartment prolapse (PMID: 23633316; PMID: 21561348). Longer-term follow-up in recurrent prolapse found more additional operations after mesh than native tissue repair at 12 years (PMID: 36662272), and a large population-based cohort found higher repeat prolapse operations after transvaginal mesh apical repair than vaginal native tissue repair (PMID: 30327850). Some cited reviews on apical prolapse provide only indirect or nonspecific support and do not explicitly report all three claim components from the abstract, so their placement as support is somewhat overstated (PMID: 37493538; PMID: 27696355; PMID: 39539032).
contradict_summary: Several contradicting items emphasize that mesh can improve anatomic outcomes or lower prolapse recurrence/failure compared with native tissue repair, without clearly showing increases in all claimed harms; for example, an updated Cochrane summary reported no differences in stress urinary incontinence or reoperation rates for prolapse/incontinence in the anterior compartment, though data were limited (PMID: 21927941), and a recent randomized trial in vault prolapse found lower composite failure with transvaginal mesh than native tissue repair and low mesh-related adverse-event rates, without abstract data showing increased bladder injury or de novo stress urinary incontinence (PMID: 38776067). Some long-term studies in recurrent or anterior prolapse reported similar overall or long-term repeat surgery when mesh-complication procedures were included, rather than a clear increase with mesh (PMID: 29167974; PMID: 27324758; PMID: 31506808). However, several articles in the CONTRADICT group actually contain partial support for the claim, noting that native tissue repair had reduced de novo stress urinary incontinence and bladder injury compared with polypropylene mesh, or that mesh increased further incontinence/prolapse surgery in some compartments (PMID: 27901278; PMID: 28010993). Other contradictory items are only indirect because they compare mesh with sacrocolpopexy rather than native tissue repair, or are narrative reviews without direct pooled estimates for the claim (PMID: 35044477; PMID: 27275813; PMID: 21857223).
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The best direct evidence comes from randomized-trial syntheses and an RCT showing increased bladder injury, de novo stress urinary incontinence, and greater combined repeat surgery burden with permanent transvaginal mesh versus native tissue repair, whereas much of the contradictory set is indirect, mixed, or misclassified.

claim: In women undergoing surgical repair of vaginal prolapse, absorbable transvaginal mesh and biological grafts do not show clear benefit over native tissue repair for prolapse-related outcomes or quality of life.
support_summary: Multiple systematic reviews and randomized trials report that biological grafts show little or no advantage over native tissue repair for prolapse symptoms, repeat surgery, recurrent prolapse, or quality of life, with evidence often low certainty (PMID: 27901278, PMID: 26858090, PMID: 23633316, PMID: 20393938). The large multicenter PROSPECT randomized trials found no meaningful improvement in prolapse symptom scores or prolapse-related quality of life at 1 or 2 years with mesh augmentation or biological grafts versus standard repair, while mesh complications occurred in more than 10% of exposed women; a related health technology assessment likewise concluded no short-term benefit in efficacy, QoL, or adverse effects for mesh inlay or biological graft versus standard repair (PMID: 28010989, PMID: 28052810). Reviews focused on apical and posterior prolapse similarly state that limited evidence does not support transvaginal mesh or graft use over native tissue repair (PMID: 37493538, PMID: 27696355, PMID: 29502352). Several longer-term studies of transvaginal mesh versus native tissue show similar subjective/functional outcomes and no reduction in repeat surgery despite occasional better anatomic results, with added mesh exposure or further operations (PMID: 29167974, PMID: 36662272, PMID: 31506808). A few supporting items are imperfectly matched because they evaluate permanent/nonabsorbable mesh rather than absorbable mesh, but their abstracts still reinforce the broader theme that symptom and quality-of-life benefits over native tissue repair are unclear.
contradict_summary: The main contradictory theme is that some evidence suggests transvaginal synthetic mesh can improve anatomic outcomes and sometimes bulge symptoms, especially in the anterior compartment, compared with native tissue repair (PMID: 27275813). However, that systematic review also explicitly states that biologic grafts and synthetic absorbable mesh did not provide an advantage in any compartment, so it only partially contradicts the claim and is partly misaligned with the specific statement (PMID: 27275813). A retrospective study of recurrent prolapse found higher composite success and lower anatomic recurrence/bulge symptoms with transvaginal mesh than repeat native tissue repair, although overall reoperation rates were similar once mesh-complication surgeries were included (PMID: 27324758). These contradictory items are weakened by focusing largely on unspecified or nonabsorbable mesh and by showing benefits mainly for anatomy rather than clear quality-of-life gains.
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence includes large randomized trials and Cochrane reviews showing no clear benefit of biological grafts or absorbable/augmented transvaginal repairs over native tissue repair for prolapse symptoms or quality of life, whereas the contradictory evidence mainly concerns other mesh types and emphasizes anatomic rather than patient-reported benefits.

claim: In people with leptospirosis, antibiotics compared with placebo or no intervention have very uncertain effects on all-cause mortality and on non-serious adverse events, and no included trial reported serious adverse events for this comparison.
support_summary: A Cochrane review of antibiotic treatment for leptospirosis found seven randomized trials, with four placebo/no-intervention comparisons, and concluded that evidence was insufficient to determine whether antibiotics affect mortality; trials were high risk of bias and adverse events did not show significant differences, although the abstract does not clearly state that serious adverse events were unreported (PMID: 22336839). A newer Cochrane review on antibiotic prophylaxis in leptospirosis similarly reported low- to very low-certainty evidence for mortality and non-serious adverse events versus placebo, and explicitly stated that none of the included trials reported serious adverse events (PMID: 38483067). However, PMID: 38483067 concerns prophylaxis rather than treatment of people with established leptospirosis, so its relevance to the claim is indirect and represents a scope discrepancy. Overall, the recurring theme is substantial uncertainty around antibiotic effects on mortality and adverse events, with absent serious-adverse-event reporting in at least one review.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, but the support is only moderate because one key supporting review directly addresses treatment uncertainty in leptospirosis (PMID: 22336839), while the other is a prophylaxis review with imperfect population/intervention alignment (PMID: 38483067). The available evidence mainly supports uncertainty about mortality and adverse events rather than demonstrating clear benefit or harm.

claim: In people with leptospirosis, different antibiotics compared with each other have very uncertain effects on all-cause mortality, serious adverse events, and non-serious adverse events.
support_summary: A Cochrane review of antibiotic treatment for leptospirosis found that among head-to-head antibiotic comparisons (including cephalosporin vs penicillin, doxycycline vs penicillin, and cephalosporin vs doxycycline), there were no statistically significant differences in mortality, and no therapy-related adverse events reached statistical significance; however, the included trials were few, small, and at high risk of bias, making effects uncertain (PMID: 22336839). A newer Cochrane review on antibiotic prophylaxis also reported that the single trial comparing doxycycline with azithromycin did not report mortality, found no clear differences in non-serious adverse events, and had very low-certainty evidence; no serious adverse events were reported in the included trials (PMID: 38483067). This second review is not directly about treatment in people with established leptospirosis, so its relevance to the claim is indirect and represents a scope discrepancy despite pointing in the same direction of uncertainty. Overall, the recurring theme is sparse randomized evidence, very low/low certainty, and no reliable demonstration that one antibiotic is better or worse than another for key safety or mortality outcomes.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because the available randomized-review evidence consistently indicates that comparisons between antibiotics for leptospirosis do not show clear differences in mortality or adverse events and are limited by low or very low certainty. The support is tempered by the fact that one cited review concerns prophylaxis rather than treatment of established disease, but no direct contradictory evidence was provided.

claim: Randomized trial evidence is insufficient to support a clear benefit of antibiotic treatment for leptospirosis infection on clinically important outcomes.
support_summary: A Cochrane review of randomized trials on antibiotic treatment for leptospirosis found seven trials, all at high risk of bias, with no statistically significant mortality benefit versus placebo/no treatment, no reduction in hospital stay, and unclear effects on clinical illness duration, concluding that evidence was insufficient to advocate for or against antibiotics and that benefit remains unclear, especially in severe disease (PMID: 22336839). A newer Cochrane review on antibiotic prophylaxis likewise found low- to very low-certainty randomized evidence with little to no clear effect on clinically relevant outcomes and explicitly noted a lack of definitive rigorous trial data and lack of trials reporting clinically relevant outcomes, although this pertains to prophylaxis rather than treatment and is therefore only indirectly aligned with the claim (PMID: 38483067). Overall, the main direct theme is that randomized evidence does not show a clear clinically important benefit, with imprecision, bias, and limited outcome reporting weakening confidence.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The strongest direct evidence is the treatment-focused Cochrane review showing no clear benefit of antibiotics on key clinical outcomes and concluding that randomized evidence is insufficient, while the prophylaxis review is consistent in spirit but only indirectly relevant to treatment.

claim: In older adults with depression residing in long-term care facilities, psychological therapies compared with usual care or other non-therapy control conditions may reduce depressive symptoms, but the evidence is very uncertain.
support_summary: Most supporting items are indirect because they study broader adult populations, dementia/MCI, stroke, or workplace settings rather than older adults with depression in long-term care facilities. The closest evidence comes from dementia-focused reviews that included some nursing home or institutional participants and found small reductions in depressive symptoms with psychological interventions versus usual care/control (e.g., SMD -0.22 in 6 RCTs, PMID: 24449085; slight benefit for CBT-based approaches but little/no effect for supportive counselling, PMID: 35466396), and music-based therapeutic interventions in mostly institutional dementia populations also probably slightly improved depressive symptoms versus usual care (PMID: 40049590). Other indirect reviews also suggest psychological therapy may reduce depression but with low or very low certainty, including post-stroke depression (PMID: 31989584; PMID: 37417452), third-wave CBT for acute depression (PMID: 24142810), and a single low-certainty dance movement therapy trial (PMID: 37549216). Several abstracts explicitly emphasize uncertainty, small effects, heterogeneity, and risk of bias, which is consistent with the claim's "may reduce" and "very uncertain" framing. A discrepancy is that some cited studies report moderate-certainty evidence or are not in long-term care populations, so they support the direction of effect more than the exact setting/certainty stated in the claim.
contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided. Within the supporting set, however, some abstracts contain signals that not all psychological approaches are effective: supportive and counselling interventions in dementia may have little or no effect on depressive symptoms (PMID: 35466396), and music-based interventions versus other active activities showed no clear effect on depressive symptoms (PMID: 40049590). These are better interpreted as within-group heterogeneity and indirect limitations rather than direct contradiction of the claim.
conclusion: Overall, the claim is more supported than contradicted. The evidence base suggests psychological therapies can modestly reduce depressive symptoms, but most evidence is indirect for long-term care residents and often low/very low certainty, so the claim's cautious conclusion of possible benefit with very uncertain evidence is justified.

claim: In older adults with depression residing in long-term care facilities, psychological therapies compared with usual care or other non-therapy control conditions may improve quality of life and psychological well-being in the short term, but the evidence is very uncertain.
support_summary: Two Cochrane reviews in people with dementia/MCI found that CBT-based or broader psychological interventions versus usual care/active control produced small short-term improvements in depressive symptoms, and one updated review also found probable improvement in patient quality of life, though most studies were community-based and not specifically long-term care residents (PMID: 35466396, PMID: 24449085). A review of music-based therapeutic interventions in mostly institutionalized people with dementia found slight short-term improvement in depressive symptoms versus usual care, but emotional well-being/quality-of-life effects were low or very low certainty and generally not clearly improved (PMID: 40049590). These studies broadly align with the idea of possible short-term benefit but are indirect for the claim because populations often had dementia rather than diagnosed depression in long-term care, and settings were mixed rather than exclusively long-term care. One listed support item is a clear mismatch: a review of behavioural interventions for medically unexplained symptoms in primary care is not directly relevant to depressed older long-term care residents, although it suggests some short-term psychological benefit in other adult populations (PMID: 32975190). Overall, the supporting literature suggests possible short-term gains in mood and sometimes quality of life, but certainty and direct applicability to the claimed population are limited.
contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is also only indirectly relevant: a Cochrane review of low-vision rehabilitation in visually impaired adults, many of them older, found very low-certainty evidence of no clear benefit of psychological therapies/group programmes on health-related quality of life, though some benefit on vision-related quality of life was noted in certain comparisons (PMID: 31985055). This does not directly address older adults with depression living in long-term care facilities and therefore serves as weak contradiction at best. Its placement in the contradict group is somewhat discrepant because the abstract emphasizes uncertainty and mixed findings rather than a direct refutation of psychological therapy benefits in the target population.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, but only modestly. The best available evidence suggests possible short-term improvement in depressive symptoms and sometimes quality of life with psychological or related therapeutic interventions, yet most evidence is indirect for long-term care residents with depression and often remains low-certainty or very uncertain.

claim: In older adults with depression residing in long-term care facilities, psychological therapies compared with non-therapy control conditions may increase treatment dropout, suggesting possible lower acceptability, but the evidence is very uncertain.
support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
contradict_summary: One review reported that psychosocial interventions for adult survivors of rape/sexual assault may not increase treatment dropout versus inactive controls (RR 0.85, 95% CI 0.51 to 1.44), but this evidence comes from a substantially different population and clinical context than older adults with depression in long-term care, limiting its relevance to the claim (PMID: 37795783). The abstract itself notes low-certainty evidence and high attrition, so even within that population the finding is not definitive (PMID: 37795783). Thus, this item is a mismatch/discrepancy rather than strong direct contradiction of the claim.
conclusion: The evidence is unclear because there is no direct supporting evidence here and the single contradicting item is only weakly informative due to major differences in population and setting. Overall, the claim is neither meaningfully supported nor convincingly contradicted by the provided evidence.

claim: In adults undergoing cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass, prophylactic corticosteroids probably do not reduce all-cause mortality compared with placebo or no treatment.
support_summary: Two systematic reviews/meta-analyses of randomized trials consistently report no mortality benefit from prophylactic corticosteroids in adults undergoing cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass. An earlier meta-analysis of 54 RCTs (3615 patients) found no reduction in mortality with corticosteroids versus placebo/no treatment (pooled OR 1.12, 95% CI 0.65-1.92) and concluded there was no beneficial effect on mortality, cardiac, or pulmonary complications (PMID: 21563145). A larger and more recent meta-analysis of 62 RCTs (16,457 patients), including the major SIRS and DECS trials, likewise found no significant mortality difference (OR 0.96, 95% CI 0.81-1.14) and explicitly stated that prophylactic corticosteroids did not reduce mortality; this review rated the mortality evidence as moderate certainty (PMID: 31972000). Across both reviews, the recurring theme is that corticosteroids may affect some secondary outcomes, but not all-cause mortality.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. This judgment is based on two concordant meta-analyses of randomized trials, including a large contemporary review with moderate-certainty evidence, both showing no significant reduction in all-cause mortality with prophylactic corticosteroids.

claim: In adults undergoing cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass, prophylactic corticosteroids may increase myocardial complications but may reduce pulmonary complications compared with placebo or no treatment.
support_summary: A large systematic review and meta-analysis of 62 randomized trials (16,457 patients) found that prophylactic corticosteroids in adults undergoing cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass were associated with a higher incidence of myocardial adverse events (OR 1.17, 95% CI 1.03-1.33) and a lower incidence of pulmonary adverse events (OR 0.86, 95% CI 0.75-0.98) versus placebo, directly matching the claim (PMID: 31972000). The abstract also notes that the clinical significance of the myocardial signal is uncertain because myocardial endpoints varied substantially across trials (PMID: 31972000).
contradict_summary: An earlier meta-analysis of 54 randomized studies (3,615 patients), described as mostly limited quality, found no clear effect of prophylactic corticosteroids on myocardial complications (OR 0.95, 95% CI 0.57-1.60) or pulmonary complications (OR 0.83, 95% CI 0.49-1.40), and concluded there was no beneficial effect on cardiac or pulmonary complications overall (PMID: 21563145). This mainly contradicts the claim's suggestion of increased myocardial complications, while the point estimate for pulmonary complications trends in the same direction as the claim but is not statistically conclusive, making this item somewhat mixed rather than purely contradictory (PMID: 21563145).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence is the newer, much larger meta-analysis showing increased myocardial adverse events and reduced pulmonary adverse events with corticosteroids, whereas the contradictory evidence is older, smaller, and largely indicates no significant effect rather than the opposite.

claim: In adults undergoing cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass, prophylactic corticosteroids may reduce infectious complications compared with placebo or no treatment, while effects on gastrointestinal bleeding, renal failure, and hospital length of stay are little to no difference or very uncertain.
support_summary: One large systematic review/meta-analysis of 62 RCTs (16,457 patients) found that prophylactic corticosteroids in adult cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass reduced surgical site infection (OR 0.81, 95% CI 0.73-0.90) and also reduced atrial fibrillation and pulmonary adverse events, while showing no mortality benefit (PMID: 31972000). This supports the claim that corticosteroids may reduce infectious complications. The abstract does not report clear renal failure results and does not provide specific quantitative findings for gastrointestinal bleeding or hospital length of stay, so support for those parts of the claim is limited from the abstract alone (PMID: 31972000).
contradict_summary: An earlier meta-analysis of 54 randomized studies (3,615 patients), mostly of limited quality, found no benefit of corticosteroids on mortality, cardiac complications, or pulmonary complications, and reported an increase in gastrointestinal bleeding (PMID: 21563145). This contradicts the claim insofar as the claim suggests little to no difference or uncertainty for gastrointestinal bleeding rather than possible harm. However, this abstract did not assess or report infectious complications such as surgical site infection, so it does not directly refute the claim's infection-related component (PMID: 21563145).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The main supporting evidence is a larger, more recent meta-analysis showing reduced surgical site infection, whereas the contradicting evidence is narrower and mainly raises concern about gastrointestinal bleeding without directly addressing infectious complications.

claim: In adults with coronary heart disease or heart failure, psychological interventions compared with no psychological intervention probably reduce depression and anxiety.
support_summary: A Cochrane review of 35 RCTs in adults with coronary heart disease found psychological interventions improved participant-reported depressive symptoms (SMD -0.27) and anxiety (SMD -0.24) versus usual care, though the certainty was low and heterogeneity was substantial (PMID: 28452408). A second systematic review in coronary artery disease patients with comorbid depression reported that psychological interventions may reduce end-of-treatment depression symptoms, again with low-certainty evidence, but it did not address anxiety and was limited to depressed CAD populations rather than all CHD or heart failure patients (PMID: 34910821). Overall, the support is mainly for CHD/CAD populations and indicates symptom reduction, but the abstracts emphasize uncertainty, poor reporting, and heterogeneous interventions. There is also a scope discrepancy with the claim because no supporting abstract here directly covers heart failure.
contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided. Within the supporting evidence, however, uncertainty is repeatedly noted: the CHD review reported low or very low certainty for psychological outcomes and substantial heterogeneity, and the CAD depression review described only low-certainty evidence for depression reduction (PMID: 28452408; PMID: 34910821). These limitations weaken confidence but do not directly contradict the claim.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, because systematic reviews in CHD/CAD populations report reductions in depressive symptoms and, for CHD, anxiety as well. However, confidence is only moderate at best because the evidence is low certainty, heterogeneous, and does not directly cover heart failure in the provided abstracts.

claim: In adults with coronary heart disease or heart failure, psychological interventions compared with no psychological intervention may improve mental health-related quality of life but have little to no effect on physical health-related quality of life.
support_summary: One Cochrane systematic review of 35 RCTs in 10,703 adults with coronary heart disease found that psychological interventions versus usual care improved psychological symptoms, including depression, anxiety, and stress, but did not improve most clinical outcomes such as total mortality, revascularization, or non-fatal myocardial infarction (PMID: 28452408). This supports the claim only indirectly, because the abstract reports symptom outcomes rather than mental or physical health-related quality-of-life domains explicitly. The review therefore aligns with possible benefit for mental well-being, while providing no direct abstract-level evidence that physical health-related quality of life improves, which is compatible with little to no physical HRQoL effect. Overall, the supporting evidence is limited by indirectness and the authors' stated uncertainty due to low/very low GRADE ratings and heterogeneity.
contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, but only moderately. The available evidence suggests psychological interventions in coronary heart disease improve mental health-related outcomes while not clearly improving broader nonpsychological outcomes, yet direct abstract-level evidence on mental versus physical health-related quality of life is limited.

claim: In adults with coronary heart disease or heart failure, psychological interventions compared with no psychological intervention probably have little to no effect on all-cause mortality and may have little to no effect on major adverse cardiovascular events.
support_summary: A Cochrane review of 35 RCTs in coronary heart disease found no evidence that psychological interventions reduced total mortality (RR 0.90, 95% CI 0.77 to 1.05), revascularisation, or non-fatal MI, although cardiac mortality was lower and psychological symptoms improved; this directly supports little to no effect on all-cause mortality and many cardiovascular events, while also containing a potentially contradictory signal for cardiac mortality (PMID: 28452408). A broader Cochrane review of meditation in adults at high risk of or with established cardiovascular disease reported very little information on clinical cardiovascular endpoints and substantial heterogeneity, offering only indirect, weak support that such interventions may have limited detectable effect on hard outcomes (PMID: 38358047). A systematic review of social network/social support interventions in people with heart disease reported no clear effect on all-cause mortality, but this intervention type is not clearly the same as psychological intervention, so it is only indirect and lower-relevance support (PMID: 37378598). Overall, the main direct evidence is the CHD Cochrane review, with the other two studies providing weaker, partly mismatched support.
contradict_summary: There are no evidence items formally listed in the contradicting group. However, the main supporting review also reported a reduction in cardiac mortality, which is a potentially conflicting finding relative to the claim that psychological interventions may have little to no effect on major adverse cardiovascular events, though this result was accompanied by low-certainty evidence and uncertainty about robustness (PMID: 28452408).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest and most direct evidence in CHD shows no clear benefit for all-cause mortality and no clear reduction in several cardiovascular morbidity outcomes, although an uncertain signal for reduced cardiac mortality prevents the evidence from being completely one-sided.

claim: In adults with Parkinson's disease, most types of physical exercise improve motor signs and quality of life compared with passive control, with the exact exercise type appearing less important than physical exercise in general.
support_summary: One Cochrane systematic review of 32 randomized trials in Parkinson's disease found that exercise interventions, considered across all types, probably reduce falls and may slightly improve health-related quality of life compared with control interventions such as usual care or sham exercise (PMID: 35665915). The abstract does support a general benefit of exercise versus passive/non-therapeutic control, but its primary focus is fall prevention rather than motor signs, so support for motor improvement is indirect rather than explicit (PMID: 35665915). It also notes that further research is needed to determine the relative impact of different exercise types, which is only weakly consistent with the claim that exercise type may be less important than exercising in general (PMID: 35665915).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is somewhat supported, but only weakly by the provided evidence. The available review supports exercise in general versus control for falls and slight quality-of-life benefit in Parkinson's disease, yet it does not directly establish broad motor-sign improvement across most exercise types or definitively show that exercise modality is unimportant.

claim: Among childbearing women receiving midwife continuity of care models versus other models of care during pregnancy and birth, midwife continuity of care models are associated with more spontaneous vaginal births and fewer caesarean sections.
support_summary: One Cochrane systematic review of 11 randomized trials including 12,276 women found that midwife-led care increased spontaneous vaginal birth (RR 1.04, 95% CI 1.02 to 1.06) and reduced instrumental delivery, regional analgesia, episiotomy, and antenatal hospitalization (PMID: 18843666). This directly supports the claim's spontaneous vaginal birth component. However, the abstract does not explicitly report caesarean section rates, so support for the 'fewer caesarean sections' part of the claim is indirect/incomplete rather than direct (PMID: 18843666).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, because a sizable randomized-trial meta-analysis supports increased spontaneous vaginal birth with midwife-led care (PMID: 18843666). However, the available abstract does not directly show fewer caesarean sections, so support for that part of the claim remains limited.

claim: Among childbearing women receiving midwife continuity of care models versus other models of care during pregnancy and birth, midwife continuity of care models likely reduce instrumental vaginal birth and may reduce episiotomy.
support_summary: One Cochrane systematic review of 11 randomized trials including 12,276 women found that midwife-led models of care were associated with lower rates of both instrumental delivery (RR 0.86, 95% CI 0.78 to 0.96) and episiotomy (RR 0.82, 95% CI 0.77 to 0.88) compared with other care models, directly supporting the claim (PMID: 18843666). The abstract also reports related benefits such as higher spontaneous vaginal birth and lower use of regional analgesia, which is consistent with reduced intervention overall (PMID: 18843666). Although the claim specifically refers to midwife continuity of care, the review is framed more broadly as 'midwife-led models of care,' so this is a minor scope mismatch rather than a clear discrepancy (PMID: 18843666).
contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The available evidence includes a large systematic review of randomized trials showing statistically significant reductions in both instrumental delivery and episiotomy with midwife-led care, though the exact wording of 'continuity of care' is somewhat broader in the cited abstract.

claim: Deployment of wMel-Wolbachia-carrying Aedes aegypti mosquitoes in an urban dengue-endemic setting probably reduces the risk of virologically confirmed dengue infection in people living in the intervention area.
support_summary: The strongest supporting evidence comes from field studies in urban endemic settings showing reduced dengue after wMel deployments: a quasi-experimental study in Niterói reported a 69% reduction in dengue incidence (PMID: 34252106), a spatiotemporal analysis in Rio found a 38% reduction despite incomplete introgression (PMID: 36182679), and city-wide Colombian deployments were associated with 95-97% reductions in notified dengue plus lower odds of virologically confirmed/presumptive dengue in treated neighborhoods (PMID: 38032857). The AWED randomized trial in Yogyakarta is repeatedly referenced across multiple abstracts as showing major protection against virologically confirmed dengue, including disruption of focal transmission and protection across all detected serotypes/genotypes (PMID: 35701454; PMID: 39543157), which is especially relevant to the claim. Additional support comes from mechanistic and entomological studies showing wMel reduces mosquito vector competence, saliva infectivity, viral replication, and maintains stable field establishment over time (PMID: 26115104; PMID: 24587459; PMID: 35196357). Several other items are only indirect support because they are protocols, models, reviews, or laboratory studies rather than direct human outcome studies, and some support items concern other Wolbachia strains such as wAlbB rather than wMel, which is a discrepancy with the claim.
contradict_summary: Only one contradicting item was provided, and it is an indirect laboratory reanalysis rather than a human field-effectiveness study. This paper reported that wMel increased mean and variance of Aedes aegypti susceptibility to dengue infection in Brazil and Vietnam genetic backgrounds, implying that higher mean susceptibility could enhance transmission in the absence of other processes (PMID: 29662096). However, the same abstract also notes that increased variance could still leave open the possibility of reduced transmission under natural selection, so the contradiction is partial and mechanistic rather than direct evidence against reduced human dengue risk after deployment. This creates a discrepancy with the much larger body of field evidence showing reduced dengue incidence after wMel releases.
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. Multiple real-world urban deployment studies, including evidence linked to the AWED randomized trial and large quasi-experimental city-wide releases, indicate reduced dengue risk after wMel deployment, whereas the sole contradicting study is a mechanistic mosquito analysis that does not directly assess virologically confirmed human dengue outcomes.

claim: wMel-Wolbachia-carrying Aedes aegypti mosquitoes can remain at high prevalence in intervention clusters over multiple years after deployment, although comparative evidence for sustained population replacement is limited.
support_summary: Multiple field studies report successful establishment and persistence of wMel in Aedes aegypti after releases, with long-term monitoring showing high or self-sustaining prevalence in several settings, including Pacific Island deployments (PMID: 38484041), Yogyakarta (PMID: 32302295), and large parts of Colombia where most monitored areas had >60% prevalence (PMID: 38032856). A decade-long follow-up in Cairns found wMel phenotypically and genomically stable up to 9–10 years post-release, supporting long-term persistence of population replacement once established (PMID: 35196357). Modeling papers also support the plausibility of durable persistence and area-wide spread over years, though these are indirect rather than empirical comparative evidence (PMID: 33892579; PMID: 28411063). Across the supporting studies, the recurring theme is that wMel can remain established for years after transient deployment, but most abstracts do not provide strong comparative evidence between intervention and control clusters specifically, matching the claim's caveat that such comparative evidence is limited.
contradict_summary: Several studies indicate that sustained high prevalence is not guaranteed and can be highly setting-dependent. In Rio de Janeiro, wMel introgression was suboptimal and remained around 32% on average up to 29 months after release, with evidence that insecticide-driven population crashes and fitness costs impaired long-term persistence (PMID: 36182679; PMID: 40700442). A follow-up study from Medellín reported much lower than previously reported Wolbachia prevalence two years after releases, with estimated individual infection rates roughly 9.5% to 33.2% and a highly heterogeneous spatial distribution, suggesting possible decline after release completion (PMID: 39366500); this directly conflicts with the more favorable earlier Colombia report (PMID: 38032856). One listed contradiction study concerns wMelPop rather than wMel and therefore is only indirectly relevant to this claim, since it shows failure of a different Wolbachia strain rather than the deployed wMel strain (PMID: 26510523).
conclusion: The statement is overall supported, but with important limitations. The evidence shows that wMel can remain established at high prevalence for multiple years in several deployment settings, yet contradictory real-world data from Rio and later Medellín indicate that sustained population replacement is not uniform across sites, so the claim's caution that comparative evidence is limited is well justified.

claim: Evidence from randomized trials is currently limited to one completed study, so the effectiveness of Wolbachia-carrying Aedes mosquito deployment in other dengue-endemic settings remains uncertain.
support_summary: A recent review notes that although multiple field deployments have reported reductions in dengue, the broad-scale and long-term outcomes of Wolbachia-based control remain uncertain, with challenges around establishment, sustainability, and generalizability across settings (PMID: 41043583). A Rio de Janeiro modelling study likewise states that evidence for large-scale real-world effectiveness in endemic settings is still scarce, even while observing a 38% reduction in dengue associated with releases in that city (PMID: 36182679). Together, these abstracts support the claim mainly by emphasizing scarcity of robust evidence across settings and lingering uncertainty rather than by directly documenting that only one randomized trial has been completed.
contradict_summary: Several abstracts report substantial effectiveness of Wolbachia deployments beyond a single setting, including quasi-experimental reductions in dengue in Niterói, Brazil (69%) (PMID: 34252106), Rio de Janeiro (38%) (PMID: 36182679, though categorized as support), and three Colombian cities (95-97% in interrupted time-series analyses) (PMID: 38032857), as well as reductions in Singapore using Wolbachia-mediated sterility approaches (PMID: 38342109; PMID: 39105274). The strongest direct randomized evidence described is the AWED cluster-randomized trial in Yogyakarta, which is repeatedly cited as pivotal and is accompanied by analyses showing marked reductions in dengue and disruption of transmission (PMID: 39543157; PMID: 36700745), but these do not actually refute the specific point that randomized-trial evidence may be limited to one completed study. Multiple protocol/update papers indicate additional randomized trials are ongoing or planned rather than completed, including AWED protocol/update, EVITA Dengue in Brazil, and a Singapore cluster-randomized trial (PMID: 29855331; PMID: 32450914; PMID: 35236394; PMID: 38902790), which is more consistent with the claim than their contradiction labels suggest. Overall, much of the contradicting set demonstrates observational or modeled effectiveness in several endemic settings, but there is a discrepancy because many items do not directly contradict the statement about the current scarcity of completed randomized trials and residual uncertainty in generalizing effectiveness to other settings.
conclusion: The evidence is mixed but leans toward the claim being broadly supported in its central point about limited completed randomized-trial evidence and uncertainty in extrapolating effectiveness to other dengue-endemic settings. Although many non-randomized and quasi-experimental studies report encouraging effectiveness across cities and countries, the abstracts provided indicate that only one completed randomized trial is clearly established, while several other randomized studies are still ongoing or described only as protocols.

claim: In infertile women undergoing assisted reproduction, intrauterine platelet-rich plasma compared with placebo or no intervention may increase clinical pregnancy and live birth or ongoing pregnancy, but the certainty of evidence is very low and the effect on miscarriage is uncertain.
support_summary: Three meta-analyses/systematic reviews suggest intrauterine PRP may improve assisted reproduction outcomes versus placebo/blank/no intervention. A 2020 meta-analysis found improved pregnancy-related outcomes after PRP in IVF/ICSI, although the abstract shown emphasizes chemical pregnancy and only indirectly supports the claim’s specific outcomes of clinical pregnancy and live birth/ongoing pregnancy (PMID: 32006776). A 2025 network meta-analysis in recurrent implantation failure reported PRP ranked best for clinical pregnancy and live birth, while miscarriage differences were not statistically significant, which aligns with possible benefit but uncertainty for miscarriage (PMID: 40035946). A 2023 meta-analysis of 23 studies reported significantly higher clinical pregnancy and live birth rates with PRP and lower miscarriage rates, but its stronger conclusion on miscarriage conflicts somewhat with the claim’s statement that miscarriage effects remain uncertain and may reflect inclusion of lower-quality/nonrandomized evidence (PMID: 38066425). Overall, the supportive literature consistently trends toward benefit for clinical pregnancy and live birth, but there is some discrepancy regarding miscarriage and likely underlying certainty.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because multiple reviews/meta-analyses report higher clinical pregnancy and live birth outcomes with intrauterine PRP, and one review also found no clear miscarriage difference. However, the evidence base appears limited by heterogeneity and likely study quality concerns, so the claim’s caution about very low certainty and uncertain miscarriage effect remains justified.

claim: In infertile women undergoing assisted reproduction with thin endometrium, intrauterine platelet-rich plasma compared with intrauterine granulocyte colony-stimulating factor has an uncertain effect on live birth, miscarriage, and clinical pregnancy.
support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
contradict_summary: One network meta-analysis of 25 RCTs in women with recurrent implantation failure, not specifically thin endometrium, reported statistically significant differences among intrauterine infusion treatments for clinical pregnancy and live birth, with PRP ranking above G-CSF by SUCRA for both outcomes, while miscarriage showed no significant difference across treatments (PMID: 40035946). However, the abstract emphasizes indirect ranking and calls for direct robust comparisons between PRP and other therapies, so it does not clearly establish a definitive PRP-versus-G-CSF effect estimate. This population mismatch and reliance on network rankings create a discrepancy with its categorization as straightforward contradiction to the claim about women with thin endometrium.
conclusion: The statement is not meaningfully contradicted by the available evidence. The only cited study suggests PRP may rank better than G-CSF for clinical pregnancy and live birth in recurrent implantation failure, but it does not directly address thin endometrium and provides limited direct comparative evidence, so overall the evidence remains unclear.

claim: In infertile women undergoing assisted reproduction, intraovarian platelet-rich plasma injection compared with no intervention has an uncertain effect on ongoing pregnancy and clinical pregnancy, with insufficient safety evidence.
support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
contradict_summary: Two reviews report improved reproductive outcomes with PRP, but both evaluate intrauterine PRP infusion rather than the claim’s intervention of intraovarian PRP injection, creating an important population/intervention mismatch. A systematic review/meta-analysis of 23 studies found intrauterine PRP was associated with higher clinical pregnancy and live birth rates and lower miscarriage in women undergoing assisted reproduction, including those with repeated implantation failure (PMID: 38066425). A network meta-analysis of 25 RCTs in recurrent implantation failure similarly ranked intrauterine PRP highly for clinical pregnancy and live birth versus placebo/blank control (PMID: 40035946). Neither abstract directly addresses intraovarian PRP, ongoing pregnancy specifically, or provides clear safety data, so these studies only indirectly conflict with the claim and also highlight a discrepancy in their categorization as direct contradiction.
conclusion: The statement is not clearly contradicted by the cited evidence because the available opposing studies concern intrauterine PRP infusion, not intraovarian PRP injection, and they do not directly resolve safety or ongoing pregnancy for the claimed intervention. Overall, the evidence for this exact claim remains unclear, with only indirect contradictory evidence.

claim: In adults with diabetes and kidney disease, angiotensin-converting-enzyme inhibitors compared with placebo or no treatment may reduce progression to kidney failure, while making little or no difference to all-cause death.
support_summary: Several systematic reviews/meta-analyses in diabetic kidney disease or albuminuric populations report that ACE inhibitors versus placebo reduce renal progression outcomes such as end-stage kidney disease, doubling of creatinine, or progression of albuminuria, while showing no significant overall effect on all-cause mortality (PMID: 21372254, PMID: 17054288, PMID: 22189484). In particular, one review of 50 studies in diabetic kidney disease found no significant mortality reduction for ACE inhibitors versus placebo overall (RR 0.91, 95% CI 0.71-1.17) but reported similarly beneficial renal effects across hard and surrogate renal outcomes (PMID: 17054288). Another broad review in albuminuric patients with cardiovascular risk factors found ACE inhibitors significantly reduced end-stage kidney disease and albuminuria progression versus placebo, while all-cause mortality was not significantly reduced (PMID: 21372254).
